text: The patient is positioned in steep reverse Trendelenburg at a 30-degree angle, with both legs on spreader bars and arms tucked, preparing for the robotic procedure.
The robotic system is docked to the patient, and the surgeon uses a third robotic arm to elevate the liver, preparing to use it as a hiatal retractor.
The surgeon begins the procedure by dividing the gastropathic ligament, a common initial step in foregut surgeries.

The surgeon uses a Cartier grasping atraumatic instrument in the left hand and a vessel sealer extend as the energy source in the right hand during the robotic type three paraesophageal hernia repair.
The robotic third arm is actively used to elevate the liver with a small grasping retractor, preparing to dissect and elevate the hiatus.
The surgeon begins dissecting the hernia sac at the twelve o'clock position on the hiatus, incising the peritoneum and using blunt dissection to identify the proper anatomical plane.
The surgeon utilizes a non-robotic assistant port with an air seal trocar, while the assistant reaches into the surgical field using a paddle Babcock clamp.

The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to elevate the hiatus at the twelve o'clock position for better exposure, while the assistant maneuvers the paddle babcock. The surgeon bluntly dissects the hernia sac, separating it from the mediastinum.

The surgeon begins dissection anteriorly, dividing the gastrophrenic and esophageal phrenic ligaments. The dissection progresses towards the base of the right crus before transitioning to the left crus.
The assistant adjusts their grip on the hernia sac to facilitate dissection progress, while the third robotic arm actively maneuvers to enhance exposure of the surgical field.
The descending thoracic aorta is visible in the center of the screen as the surgeon continues to divide the hernia sac, staying just inside the chlora to minimize deep peritonealization.

The surgeon reaches the limit of dissection at the base of the left crus and returns to the right crus, attempting to develop a retroesophageal window to connect the bases of the left and right crura. The presence of a large paraesophageal hernia may impede this connection.

The surgeon divides the short gastric vessels using a vessel sealer, while the assistant holds the anterior wall of the stomach with a Babcock clamp. The surgeon's left hand retracts the gastro-splenic omentum to facilitate the procedure.

The surgeon uses a pointer at the console to guide the trainee in taking down the short gastric vessels, maintaining a distance of approximately one centimeter from the greater curvature of the stomach.
The surgeon liberally mobilizes the short gastric vessels to achieve tension-free mobilization of the gastric fundus, preparing for a Nissen fundoplication.
The assistant actively re-grasps the stomach while the surgeon divides the short gastric vessels during a partial anterior 180-degree fundoplication, known as the Dor fundoplication.
The camera port is positioned slightly more towards the left side of the patient, just below the costal margin in the midclavicular line, providing a view for the surgical procedure.
The surgeon approaches the proximal stomach, with the short gastric vessels visibly divided, allowing access to the remaining hernia sac.

The surgeon continues to divide the attachments of the hernia sac, visualizing the hiatus as a clock face. The dissection progresses along the right crus from twelve o'clock to six o'clock, and then along the left crus from twelve to four o'clock, before switching techniques to further divide the hernia sac.

The assistant holds the base of the anterior vagus nerve, visible in the top center of the screen, while the surgeon divides the hernia sac up to the insertion point of the vagus nerve onto the proximal stomach, remaining on the patient's left side.

The surgeon maintains the patency of the anterior vagus nerve while avoiding the lesser curvature and associated vasculature. The pronounced hernia sac and anterior gastric fat pad are being divided and freed from the proximal stomach and its surrounding structures.

The assistant removes the freed hernia sac from the surgical field. The gastroesophageal junction is visible, positioned in the mediastinum, indicating the need for further mobilization of the esophagus to achieve adequate intra-abdominal length.

The surgeon has repositioned the third arm anteriorly in the hiatus to begin mediastinal dissection. The dissection is divided into thirds: the anterior third from ten o'clock to two o'clock on the esophagus, the left posterolateral third from two o'clock to six o'clock, and the right posterolateral third from six o'clock to ten o'clock.

The surgeon transitions from the anterior third dissection to the left posterolateral dissection, carefully avoiding the pleura located off-screen to the left. An assistant uses a low-profile instrument, possibly a suction irrigator, to hold the retro esophageal window at the top right of the screen. The surgeon then completes the right posterolateral third dissection, revealing the esophageal mesentery.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the esophageal aortic mesentery, with direct perforators to the esophagus visible in the bottom right of the screen. The thoracic spine vertebral bodies are also visible in the same area. The pleura is pointed out, and care is taken to avoid entering it while continuing to dissect the remaining esophageal mesentery.
The surgeon enlarges a hole in the pleura to prevent tension pneumothorax, revealing the aorta adjacent to the thoracic spine vertebral bodies.

The robotic third arm elevates the twelve o'clock position of the hiatus, while the other two arms triangulate to enhance exposure and access. The wristed instruments offer dexterity and flexibility for precise manipulation within the mediastinum.

The assistant retracts the retroesophageal window using a suction irrigator. The surgeon places an Ethibond suture in a figure-of-eight pattern to re-approximate the posterior crura, distributing tension across the crura to prevent the suture from pulling through. The surgeon uses a sliding knot technique followed by manual knot placement, noting the thinness of the crura which may require reinforcement.

The esophagus is visible gently positioned over the posterior crural closure. The surgeon avoids using more than two figure-of-eight sutures to prevent distortion of the esophageal trajectory as it exits the mediastinum.
The surgeon prepares a blunt tip Hurst bougie for fundoplication, selecting the bougie size based on the patient's body habitus, with sizes ranging from fifty-four to sixty French.

The surgeon prepares a Phasix ST mesh, which is a slowly resorbing mesh designed to be absorbed by the body within nine to twelve months. The mesh is seven by ten centimeters in size and has a U-shaped notch cut on its interior edge.
The surgeon uses interrupted Oethaban sutures to tack the mesh in place at the diaphragm, avoiding non-absorbable tackers due to proximity to the heart, pericardium, and other mobile structures.

The surgeon tacks the mesh anteriorly and places another tack in the retroesophageal window where the crura were re-approximated. The mesh is secured with interrupted sutures at its edges to keep it flat and prevent folding before incorporation occurs.

The surgeon makes a relaxing incision on the left hemidiaphragm due to excessive tension, covering the opening with a piece of nonabsorbable plastic mesh, specifically Ventrolite. Subsequently, a biologic mesh is placed around the hiatus.

The surgeon has secured the Phasix ST mesh superiorly and inferiorly, ensuring it is adequately in place. An interior gap remains visible, which will be addressed shortly. The surgeon visually closes the hiatus to approximate the esophagus circumferentially without using a bougie for calibration.
The surgeon places a blunt-tipped Hearst bougie into the proximal stomach, ensuring minimal risk of injury during the procedure.

The assistant uses a Babcock clamp to grasp the greater curvature of the stomach through the retroesophageal window, approximately eight centimeters from the angle of His. The fundus is brought back around, and the surgeon performs a shoeshine maneuver to ensure the wrap is not twisted and has adequate mobility. The surgeon then adjusts the grasp to a more distal part of the greater curvature, taking a bite of the fundus, a slip of the anterior esophagus, and then the fundus, following the standard Nissen fundoplication technique.
The surgeon secures a slip of the esophagus with two of the three wrap stitches, demonstrating a generous intra-abdominal esophageal length.

The robotic tools measure a distance of four centimeters from the left crus to the angle of His. Interrupted Oetaban sutures are used to create the wrap, with the current stitch being counted as the middle stitch. A lower stitch is placed without including the esophagus, as it is positioned at the gastroesophageal junction. The surgeon employs a slipknot technique.

The surgeon begins with a surgeon's throw, followed by a simple throw in the same direction, using their hand to slide the knot down. This is followed by another simple knot in the opposite direction, alternating the throws to secure the suture.

The surgeon places the third wrap stitch above the first one, ensuring a secure bite of the stomach while avoiding any hernia sac or residual fat. The stitches are positioned within a two-centimeter distance to prevent excessive length of the wrap, which could lead to dysphagia.
After placing the third wrap stitch, the assistant removes the bougie from the esophagus. The surgeon observes an anterior gap and proceeds to place anterior coronoplasty stitches to close this gap.
The surgeon uses mesh to reinforce the coronal closure, ensuring it is not too tight to prevent postoperative dysphagia. A minimal visible gap is left to avoid complications.

The surgeon performs an anterior gastropexy by suturing the gastric wrap to the diaphragm using stitches from the fundus to the left crus. The first pexy stitch is placed at the two o'clock position at the hiatus to prevent recurrence.
The surgeon places a pexy stitch from the right crus to the right side of the wrap, securing the fundus to the hiatus. The reconstruction of the hiatus and fundoplication is visually complete.

The surgeon checks for hemostasis, noting the surgical field is dry. The liver is lowered, and a small grasping retractor is removed through the liver retractor port. A XV French drain is placed into the mediastinum to manage potential seroma fluid, tucked under the liver and exiting through an existing port site.

The surgeon uses the robotic system's tile pro feature to display a secondary image during routine intraoperative endoscopy. The endoscopic view shows the wrap, ensuring it is not twisted, and documents the hill grade of the valve with the stomach fully insufflated, flattening the rugae.
The surgeon displays a Hill grade one valve, resembling a stack of coins or an omega shape, indicating a successful wrap. The procedure concludes with the desufflation of the stomach.
